title: Shared Parental Leave Regulations 2014
chapters: SI 2014/3050 · In Force 1 December 2014 (for babies due on or after 5 April 2015) · Made under ERA 1996 ss.75E–75K · Supplemented by Statutory Shared Parental Pay (General) Regulations 2014 (SI 2014/3051)
desc: The Shared Parental Leave Regulations 2014 allow eligible parents to share up to 50 weeks of leave and 37 weeks of pay (ShPP) between them in the first year after a child's birth or placement for adoption. The mother must curtail her maternity leave (or maternity allowance) to create the shared parental leave entitlement. The parents can then share the remaining leave and pay between them — they can take leave at the same time, take it in turns, or in discontinuous blocks (subject to employer agreement for discontinuous leave). Each parent must give at least 8 weeks' notice of their intention to take shared parental leave. Qualifying conditions: both parents must be eligible (26 weeks' continuous employment at the 15th week before the expected week of childbirth for the employee parent; the other parent must have worked for at least 26 of the 66 weeks before the expected week of childbirth and earned at least £390 in total in 13 of those weeks). ShPP is payable at the statutory rate or 90% of average weekly earnings, whichever is lower. Take-up of SPL has been low (estimated 2–3% of eligible families), attributed to complexity, financial constraints, and cultural norms.

parts.title: Shared Parental Leave Entitlement and Administration

sections.title: Eligibility, curtailment, booking, and discontinuous leave
sections.summary: The SPL framework operates in three stages: (1) Curtailment: the mother must curtail her maternity leave by giving a curtailment notice. The curtailment notice must specify the date on which maternity leave will end. Once given, it is generally binding. (2) Notice of entitlement: each parent who intends to take SPL must give their employer a notice of entitlement and intention at least 8 weeks before the first period of SPL begins. The notice must state the total amount of SPL available, the dates of leave intended to be taken, and non-binding indications of any further leave. (3) Period of leave notice: a notice specifying the dates of a period of SPL. An employee can give up to three period of leave notices per pregnancy. Continuous leave (a single unbroken block) must be granted by the employer. Discontinuous leave (multiple blocks with working periods between them) requires the employer's agreement — if the employer does not agree within 2 weeks, the employee can take the total amount requested as a single continuous block starting on the date specified (or an agreed alternative date).
sections.text:
Key features of the SPL framework:

• Total shared entitlement: up to 50 weeks' leave and 37 weeks' ShPP (52 weeks maternity leave minus the 2-week compulsory maternity leave; 39 weeks SMP minus 2 weeks)
• Mother must curtail maternity leave to create the shared entitlement
• Both parents can take leave at the same time
• Each parent may give up to 3 'period of leave' notices per pregnancy
• Continuous leave requests must be granted
• Discontinuous leave requests require employer agreement — if not agreed within 14 days, the employee can take the leave as a single continuous block
• 8 weeks' notice required for each period of leave
• ShPP payable at prescribed rate (£194.32/week from April 2026) or 90% of AWE, whichever is lower
• Right to return: after SPL, the employee is entitled to return to the same job (if total leave across all types is 26 weeks or less) or a suitable alternative job (if total leave exceeds 26 weeks)
• Protection: dismissal or detriment for taking or seeking to take SPL is automatically unfair

versions.description: SPL Regulations came into force for babies due on or after 5 April 2015. Replaced additional paternity leave (which had been in force from 2011).
ERA 2025 s.16–17: removes the restriction on taking paternity leave after SPL, giving parents more flexibility in sequencing their leave.

leadingCases.name: Ali v Capita Customer Management Ltd [2019] EWCA Civ 900
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal held that paying enhanced maternity pay to mothers but only statutory shared parental pay to fathers taking SPL does not constitute direct sex discrimination. Maternity leave and SPL serve different purposes — maternity leave protects the health of the mother and facilitates breastfeeding, while SPL is about childcare. A man taking SPL is not in comparable circumstances to a woman on maternity leave.
leadingCases.significance: Key authority — enhanced maternity pay vs statutory ShPP for fathers is not direct sex discrimination

leadingCases.name: Hextall v Chief Constable of Leicestershire Police [2022] UKEAT/0139/21
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT upheld the Ali v Capita approach and confirmed that enhanced shared parental pay need not match enhanced maternity pay. The different purposes of maternity leave and SPL justify different pay treatment.
leadingCases.significance: Reinforced Ali v Capita — enhanced maternity pay vs SPL pay differential is lawful.

leadingCases.name: Price v Powys County Council [2021] UKEAT/0253/20
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT considered the notification requirements for SPL and held that an employer's refusal to allow discontinuous leave must be communicated within the 14-day statutory period, after which the employee may take the leave as a single continuous block.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified the procedural requirements for discontinuous SPL requests and the 14-day employer response window.

refs: ERA 1996 ss.75E–75K (shared parental leave enabling provisions)
MPL 1999 (maternity leave — curtailment is the gateway to SPL)
PAL 2002 (paternity leave — ERA 2025 removes restriction on taking after SPL)

pendingAmendments: ERA 2025 ss.16-17 — paternity leave after SPL restriction removed (6 April 2026)
Government review of SPL framework — low take-up acknowledged but no reform announced

leadingCases.name: Ali v Capita Customer Management [2019] EWCA Civ 900
leadingCases.heldText: Enhanced maternity pay without matching SPL pay is not direct sex discrimination. Maternity leave and SPL serve different purposes.
leadingCases.significance: The defining authority on the SPL/maternity pay differential question.

leadingCases.name: Hextall v Chief Constable of Leicestershire [2022] UKEAT/0139/21
leadingCases.heldText: Reinforced Ali v Capita — enhanced maternity pay vs SPL pay differential is lawful.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed the Ali v Capita position at EAT level.

parliamentaryOrigin.enablingAct: Employment Rights Act 1996 ss.75E–75K (as inserted by Children and Families Act 2014)
parliamentaryOrigin.comingIntoForce: 2014-12-01 (for babies due on or after 5 April 2015)
parliamentaryOrigin.madeBy: Secretary of State for Business, Innovation and Skills
parliamentaryOrigin.note: SPL was introduced by the Coalition Government as part of the Children and Families Act 2014. It replaced additional paternity leave. The policy intent was to encourage shared caregiving. Take-up has remained low (2-3% of eligible families). ERA 2025 removes the restriction on paternity leave after SPL.
